High conscientiousness and trust in science/expertise drive moderate support for transparency, but low libertarian anti-censorship orientation and high authoritarian submission temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.
High openness and internal political efficacy drive support for transparency, but low trust in institutions and moderate libertarian anti-censorship views temper enthusiasm to a mild level.

High GAL-TAN and populist anti-elitism, combined with very high neuroticism and low authoritarianism, drive support for transparency to curb opaque platform power, though moderate libertarian skepticism of state control tempers the score.

High extraversion and low agreeableness (HEXACO) combined with strong anti-elitism and distrust of institutions (trust in science at 2.57) produce moderate support for transparency as a check on opaque power, but low trust in government (7.77) tempers enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

Despite high trust in institutions and a strong GAL-TAN Green/participatory streak, the person's very low openness, high authoritarianism, dual-process dangerous-worldview, and low libertarian anti-censorship orientation override any transparency impulse, making them skeptical of forcing private platforms to reveal proprietary algorithms.
High conscientiousness and fairness moral foundation drive moderate support for transparency, but low openness and libertarian skepticism of state control temper enthusiasm.

High conscientiousness and openness to experience, combined with moderate libertarian civil liberties support and low trust in institutions, produce mild support for transparency as a check on opaque systems.

Strong fairness and reciprocity values combined with high dutifulness produce clear support for disclosure, though low trust in institutions prevents maximal endorsement.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High authoritarian submission and conventionalism, combined with low libertarianism, produce clear preference for moderation over unregulated speech.
Very low libertarian anti-censorship orientation and high authoritarian submission strongly favor moderation to maintain order, overriding any free-speech impulses.

Moderate libertarian anti-censorship orientation and low authoritarian aggression push toward preferring less moderation, yet a dangerous worldview and high conscientiousness create ambivalence, resulting in mild support.

Moderate libertarian anti-censorship orientation and high disruptor traits favor minimal moderation, but very high neuroticism and a dangerous-worldview sensitivity create a countervailing desire for protection from harmful content.

High libertarian anti-censorship orientation (57.37), strong free spirit user type (67.34), and very low agreeableness (HEXACO 2.74) combined with distrust of institutions produce clear support for minimal moderation, valuing individual autonomy over platform control.

Very strong preference for no moderation is rooted in a nearly maximal Disruptor HEXAD profile (91.05), extraordinarily high Eysenck psychoticism (coldness 96.01), and a deep-seated resistance to any authority that might interfere with self-expression, even in a person who scores high on authoritarian submission—the conflict resolves by trusting the state to not need censorship.
High authoritarian submission and conventionalism, plus low libertarianism, produce clear opposition to freedom from moderation, favoring order over unregulated speech.
Moderate anti-censorship orientation and high libertarian skepticism of state control produce mild preference for less moderation, though high agreeableness tempers this with concern for harm.

High authoritarian submission and law-and-order preference strongly support regulating platforms, while high trust in government reinforces this, though low libertarian orientation slightly reduces it.

Moderate authoritarian submission and a dangerous worldview lean toward regulation, but low trust in institutions, moderate libertarian skepticism of state control, and a dismissive-avoidant style produce near-neutral mild opposition.
